message:
chore(beads): review-pass-5 — 4 HIGH conformance-vs-upstream gaps

Deeper /testing-conformance-harnesses audit of fp-conformance vs
the upstream pandas contract. Baseline steady (3171 passed / 0
failed / 13 ignored on the re-run; transient "1 failed" from
pass-4 confirmed as stderr-stream glitch, not a regression).

  frankenpandas-urhy [HIGH] conformance: pandas_oracle.py is
                            4985 LOC with ZERO test harness.
                            Circular trust: the "reference" is a
                            5k-line Python shim; if a normalize_*
                            helper or op_* handler has a bug, we
                            match the wrong answer and conformance
                            passes green. Fix shape: oracle/tests/
                            with test_pandas_oracle.py +
                            test_normalize.py + test_dispatch.py,
                            ~30% coverage lifts oracle from 100%
                            trust / 0% verification to something
                            measurable.

  frankenpandas-k05t [HIGH] conformance: every one of the 1249
                            fixtures is sub-4KB (min 186 / median
                            969 / max 3237 bytes). pandas parity
                            untested on any frame > ~30 rows. The
                            AG-04/AG-05 groupby-fast-path claims,
                            SIMD boundaries, int-overflow edge
                            cases, arrow batch boundaries — all
                            classes that only manifest at scale —
                            have zero conformance coverage. Fix
                            shape: Tier-S/M/L structure (PR runs
                            S+M, nightly runs L), ~50-130 new
                            parametric fixtures, no Rust source
                            changes.

  frankenpandas-zk1j [HIGH] conformance: 328 unique ops covered
                            vs pandas' ~1000+ public API surface
                            — no gap matrix answers "what is NOT
                            tested." Capability table claim "430+
                            packet suites, 1249 fixtures, all
                            green" sounds comprehensive but
                            doesn't enumerate the ~700 uncovered
                            pandas methods. Fix shape:
                            scripts/gen_pandas_api_listing.py
                            (introspect a pinned pandas install)
                            + scripts/gen_coverage_matrix.py
                            (diff vs fixture operation names) +
                            auto-generated COVERAGE_MATRIX.md
                            with Full/Partial/Zero categorization.

  frankenpandas-niwb [HIGH] conformance: error-path coverage
                            7.7% (96 / 1249 fixtures). pandas
                            exception-category parity (TypeError
                            vs KeyError vs ValueError vs IndexError)
                            is part of the contract users catch
                            on. Most ops have positive-path
                            fixtures but no error-path sibling.
                            Fix shape: new expected_error_category
                            enum field, extend oracle to capture
                            pandas exceptions, generate 2-4 error
                            fixtures per op_class (~200-400 new
                            fixtures), lift coverage to ~30% in
                            first sweep.

All four interlock with earlier review beads:
- urhy (oracle tests) + d6xa (CI pandas install) + 8irq (pandas
  pin) form the pipeline-integrity triangle. None of them works
  alone; together they make CI conformance enforceable.
- k05t (scale fixtures) + ing6 (perf regression gate) — correctness
  at scale + throughput at scale. Both needed to back the
  performance claims in README.
- zk1j (API gap matrix) + n609 (COVERAGE.md stale) + nato
  (RequirementLevel tagging) — automated matrix supersedes the
  hand-maintained COVERAGE.md and enriches it with level data.
- niwb (error coverage) + 7cfm (panic-contract rustdoc) — Rust-
  side panic contract + pandas-side error-category parity form
  the user-facing error spec.

Applied skill hooks:
- Pattern 1 Differential Testing → oracle trustworthiness
- Coverage Accounting Matrix → scale + API gap
- "Test only happy paths = most dangerous anti-pattern" → errors
- "Before claiming conformance, prove it" → API gap matrix

Running session totals after 5 review passes:
  2 CRITICAL (d6xa)
  17 HIGH    (zjme + 7cfm + 36qc + ing6 + 1d9y + qi6y + boyr +
              liai + lxhr + 0l5r + 0a83 + 8k1i + urhy + k05t +
              zk1j + niwb + pass-1 HIGH)
  4 MEDIUM
  1 LOW
= 24 review beads total.

Zero shipped-code bugs. Every finding is documented with concrete
fix shape, blast radius, and interlock commentary. Workspace
passes 3171 tests and 0 clippy warnings throughout all 5 passes.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

-{"id":"frankenpandas-fd90","title":"[EPIC] SQL backend abstraction — trait + PostgreSQL + MySQL","description":"Pandas-parity SQL IO across multiple backends. Today fp_io::read_sql / write_sql hardcode rusqlite::Connection; pandas read_sql/to_sql works with any SQLAlchemy-compatible engine (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, MS SQL, Oracle, ...). Multi-slice umbrella; file child beads per slice.

1. pub trait SqlConnection in fp-io (DBAPI2-like surface): execute_query(sql, params) -> RowIter, execute_statement(sql, params) -> usize, with_transaction<F>(f) -> Result<T>. Minimum surface to cover rusqlite, tokio-postgres, and mysql_async concrete backends behind the same trait.

2. Cargo features: sql-sqlite (default), sql-postgresql, sql-mysql. Each feature brings its backend crate and a wrapper impl SqlConnection for that backend's Connection type. Additive; disabling sql-sqlite drops the rusqlite dep.

3. read_sql / write_sql / read_sql_table / read_sql_query signatures move from rusqlite::Connection to <C: SqlConnection>. Existing SQLite call sites keep working because rusqlite::Connection gets the wrapper impl.

4. Param binding: closes frankenpandas-tk3k by letting the public surface accept params: &[&(dyn ToSqlBindable)] routed through each backend's type-specific binder.

5. chunksize streaming: read_sql(.., chunksize=Some(n)) returns impl Iterator<Item=Result<DataFrame, ...>> to match pandas.

6. coerce_float: read_sql(.., coerce_float=true) promotes NUMERIC / DECIMAL / MONEY to Float64 at row-materialization time.

7. Live-oracle conformance: packet fixtures per backend (sqlite/postgres/mysql) verifying round-trip parity with pandas' pd.read_sql(engine) for each.

Blocker for m3e8 closure: the README now honestly says 'SQL: SQLite-only via rusqlite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned' (commit-pending, br-m3e8). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing — at minimum slices (1)-(3).

Decomposition strategy: (1) is the keystone — pick between (a) a minimal DBAPI2-style trait sufficient to host all three backends without leaking backend-specific types into the public API, vs (b) a typestate / erased-impl approach. (2) feature-gates each backend behind Cargo features to keep binary size bounded. (4)-(7) then unblock incrementally behind (1)+(2)+(3).","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:41.189776401Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:38:43.534801097Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend-abstraction","epic","io","sql"]}

1. pub trait SqlConnection in fp-io (DBAPI2-like surface): execute_query(sql, params) -> RowIter, execute_statement(sql, params) -> usize, with_transaction<F>(f) -> Result<T>. Minimum surface to cover rusqlite, tokio-postgres, and mysql_async concrete backends behind the same trait.

2. Cargo features: sql-sqlite (default), sql-postgresql, sql-mysql. Each feature brings its backend crate and a wrapper impl SqlConnection for that backend's Connection type. Additive; disabling sql-sqlite drops the rusqlite dep.

3. read_sql / write_sql / read_sql_table / read_sql_query signatures move from rusqlite::Connection to <C: SqlConnection>. Existing SQLite call sites keep working because rusqlite::Connection gets the wrapper impl.

4. Param binding: closes frankenpandas-tk3k by letting the public surface accept params: &[&(dyn ToSqlBindable)] routed through each backend's type-specific binder.

5. chunksize streaming: read_sql(.., chunksize=Some(n)) returns impl Iterator<Item=Result<DataFrame, ...>> to match pandas.

6. coerce_float: read_sql(.., coerce_float=true) promotes NUMERIC / DECIMAL / MONEY to Float64 at row-materialization time.

7. Live-oracle conformance: packet fixtures per backend (sqlite/postgres/mysql) verifying round-trip parity with pandas' pd.read_sql(engine) for each.

Blocker for m3e8 closure: the README now honestly says 'SQL: SQLite-only via rusqlite; PostgreSQL/MySQL planned' (commit-pending, br-m3e8). Closing that qualifier requires this epic landing — at minimum slices (1)-(3).

Decomposition strategy: (1) is the keystone — pick between (a) a minimal DBAPI2-style trait sufficient to host all three backends without leaking backend-specific types into the public API, vs (b) a typestate / erased-impl approach. (2) feature-gates each backend behind Cargo features to keep binary size bounded. (4)-(7) then unblock incrementally behind (1)+(2)+(3).","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-04-22T12:02:41.189776401Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:04:18.364049452Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["backend-abstraction","epic","io","sql"]}

Add edge packets for null-aware diff and periods=2 pct_change, update packet smoke thresholds and coverage accounting, then verify with rch-routed fp-conformance tests/check/clippy.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-18T13:04:47.960089948Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T13:17:44.683642292Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T13:17:44.681877722Z","close_reason":"Added Series diff negative/null fixtures and pct_change periods=2 fixture with smoke coverage","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-jy6q","title":"fp-io read_csv skipfooter parameter parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T05:42:04.781969223Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T05:49:09.631846008Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T05:49:09.629987690Z","close_reason":"Added skipfooter field + truncation after nrows, 4 unit tests, clippy clean","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-k05t","title":"[HIGH] conformance: every fixture is sub-4KB — pandas parity untested on any frame size > ~30 rows","description":"Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Coverage Accounting Matrix rule to frame size distribution.

**Observation.** find + stat on crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/*.json:
  count=1249  min=186  median=969  p95=1702  max=3237  mean=992  (bytes)

Every fixture is sub-4KB. The largest fixture is 3237 bytes — ~30 rows × a handful of columns at most. Zero fixtures exercise:
- Large-frame arithmetic (10k / 100k / 1M rows)
- Group cardinality > ~10 (pandas GroupBy has distinct fast paths at different cardinalities: dense Int64 keys, range-index, small-set hash, large-cardinality hash — our impl mirrors this per README's "three automatic execution paths")
- Column-count > ~10 (arrow batch-boundary handling; pandas stress tests at 100+ cols)
- Long string values (Utf8 column of 4096-byte strings; pandas has 2GB string-array limit, ours has rust-String heap limit)
- Wide-index scenarios (frame with 50+ levels of row MultiIndex for the 1zzp.5 conformance; currently row-MI fixtures are 2-level max)

**Consequence.** The conformance claim says pandas parity, but only on toy inputs. The exact bug classes that manifest at scale — int overflow on cumulative sum, SIMD boundary conditions, group-aggregation fast-path vs slow-path divergence, memory-layout edge cases in arrow/parquet round-trips — have zero conformance coverage. The AG-04/AG-05 claim in README (87% groupby speedup via three auto execution paths) is untested against pandas on any frame big enough to hit the non-dense path.

**Skill rule violated:** Coverage Accounting Matrix requires enumerating EVERY testable requirement. "Behavior on a 1M-row frame equals pandas' behavior on the same 1M-row frame" is a requirement. Not-tested = not-covered = not-conformant.

**Severity.** HIGH — the product's performance claims are at the scale where bugs would actually matter for users; conformance is tested at the scale where performance is irrelevant. The gap between "tested" and "claimed" is three orders of magnitude.

**Fix shape:**

Tier structure (balances CI runtime vs coverage):
1. **Tier-S fixtures** (ship today): small (≤ 30 rows, ≤ 5 cols). Current 1249 fixtures all fall here. Runs every PR.
2. **Tier-M fixtures** (new): medium (1k-10k rows, 5-20 cols). Runs every PR but behind `fp-conformance-medium` feature or `--test-threads=1`. Expected: 50-100 new fixtures covering each fast-path-triggering size threshold for groupby, rolling, arithmetic.
3. **Tier-L fixtures** (new, nightly only): large (100k+ rows). Runs on scheduled nightly CI. Expected: 20-30 new fixtures covering the scale-specific divergence classes.

Generator sketch:
- `oracle/generate_scale_fixtures.py` produces parametric fixtures: for each (size_tier, op_class) pair, generate 3-5 fixtures at representative sizes. Deterministic seed so fixtures are reproducible.
- `crates/fp-conformance/tests/scale_conformance.rs` loads tier-M and tier-L fixtures and runs them through the same assert_*_expected comparators.

CI integration:
- Existing `cargo test -p fp-conformance` runs Tier-S (status quo).
- New `cargo test -p fp-conformance --features scale-m` runs Tier-S + Tier-M. Wired to PR CI.
- New scheduled nightly workflow runs Tier-S + Tier-M + Tier-L and uploads timing metrics.

Blast radius: +1 Python generator, +1 Rust test file, +50-130 fixtures (but each tiny-to-medium size — total disk growth ~5-10 MB), +1 CI workflow for nightly scale. No changes to existing fixtures.

Related: br-ing6 (perf regression gate) — scale fixtures complement it by checking correctness-at-scale, while ing6 checks throughput-at-scale. Both needed.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:10:41.578998944Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:10:41.578998944Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","fixtures","review","scale"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-k2g","title":"Add series_describe conformance coverage (FP-P2D-117)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-17T09:21:53.232308210Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-17T09:29:55.482196898Z","closed_at":"2026-04-17T09:29:55.479897857Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"frankenpandas-nat2","title":"pandas: DataFrame.to_markdown(tablefmt='simple_grid') parity","description":"Python signature: pd.DataFrame.to_markdown(index=..., tablefmt='simple_grid'). fp-frame still falls through the unsupported to_markdown tablefmt branch for simple_grid even though nearby grid-family renderers now exist. Add simple_grid rendering in crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs with exact-output regression coverage for both index=False and index=True paths, preserving existing github/pipe/grid/simple/psql/rst/rounded_grid/fancy_grid behavior.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T18:51:44.137785560Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T19:34:01.542037633Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T19:34:01.539682074Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-nato","title":"[MEDIUM] conformance: no RequirementLevel (MUST/SHOULD/MAY) tagging on fixtures — compliance score uncomputable","description":"Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's Pattern 4 (Spec-Derived Test Matrix) + ConformanceTest trait design.

**Observed state:** every fixture under crates/fp-conformance/fixtures/packets/ is treated as equal weight. Grep for 'RequirementLevel' or 'MUST' or 'requirement_level' in the harness code returns nothing. The fixture JSON schema has packet_id, case_id, mode (strict/hardened), operation, data payload, expected_* — no requirement_level field. run_packet_suite + run_e2e_suite report pass/fail counts but cannot distinguish "all MUST clauses pass" from "all MUSTs + SHOULDs + MAYs pass."

**Violates skill requirements:**
- Pattern 4 template explicitly carries level: RequirementLevel::{Must, Should, May}.
- Coverage Accounting Matrix header: "MUST Clauses | SHOULD Clauses | ... | Score" — currently impossible to populate.
- Rule: "Score < 0.95 for MUST clauses = NOT conformant." — we cannot even compute this score because level isn't tracked.

**Concrete impact:** if we ever need to ship with known MAY-level gaps (likely, pandas has many undocumented edge cases), the current harness can't tell the difference between a MUST-regression (ship-blocker) and a MAY-regression (acceptable divergence tracked in DISCREPANCIES.md).

**Fix shape:**
1. Add `requirement_level: Option<RequirementLevel>` to PacketFixture struct in fp-conformance/src/lib.rs (serde default = Must when absent — conservative default).
2. Enum RequirementLevel { Must, Should, May } with serde rename_all = "lowercase".
3. Backfill existing fixtures: most groupby / arithmetic / IO round-trip cases are MUST (pandas documents the contract). Display-width / error-message-text cases are MAY. String-locale behavior is SHOULD.
4. Update DifferentialReport + PacketSuiteReport to carry per-level pass/fail counts.
5. COVERAGE.md compliance matrix (see sibling br-frankenpandas-n609) groups by level. Fail CI when MUST score < 95%; WARN on SHOULD < 90%; ignore MAY regressions but log them.

Priority MEDIUM — infrastructure for future ship-decision clarity, not a correctness bug.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:47:03.669796789Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T18:47:03.669796789Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","requirement-level","review"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-nid","title":"Add metamorphic properties for between","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-17T07:22:21.590448526Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-17T07:35:57.859937807Z","closed_at":"2026-04-17T07:35:57.858213121Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-niwb","title":"[HIGH] conformance: error-path coverage 7.7% (96/1249 fixtures) — pandas exception-category parity untested at scale","description":"Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's "Test only happy paths / Error handling divergences are the most dangerous" anti-pattern.

**Observation.**
  fixtures with expected_error (or expected_error_contains): 96
  fixtures with expected_series or expected_frame:         1069
  fixtures total:                                          1249

Error-path conformance coverage is **7.7%** (96/1249). pandas' error surface is a substantial fraction of its behavior contract — users rely on catching specific exception types (TypeError for wrong dtype, KeyError for missing label, ValueError for shape mismatch, IndexError for out-of-range integer position).

**Concrete pandas error-semantics examples NOT covered by our 96 error fixtures (sampled from pandas docs):**
- `df[nonexistent_col]` → KeyError with specific message template; we raise FrameError::CompatibilityRejected with different text.
- `df.loc[key]` on a MultiIndex with wrong arity → TypeError vs our Result::Err shape.
- `df.join(other, how='invalid_strategy')` → ValueError; we probably accept or ignore.
- `df.iloc[10000]` on a 10-row frame → IndexError; we should return Err but haven't locked the shape.
- `pd.concat([], axis=0)` → "No objects to concatenate" ValueError; we may panic or return empty frame silently.
- `.groupby([])` (empty-by) → ValueError; unverified.
- Integer / float coercion impossibilities: `pd.Series(["a"]).astype(int)` → ValueError; we return Err with different text.

**DISC-003 says:** "tests check error category, not message text" — which is exactly the right call. BUT: most of the 1153 positive-path fixtures don't have an error-path sibling. A fixture like `FP-P2D-050 series_add_basic_strict` covers `series.add(other)` on valid inputs; where is `FP-P2D-050-err_dtype series_add_mixed_dtype_strict` showing what happens when lhs is Int64 and rhs is Utf8?

**Skill rule violated:** "Test only happy paths → Error handling divergences are the most dangerous." Ranked #7 in the skill's anti-patterns.

**Severity.** HIGH — pandas-compatible error semantics are part of the parity claim. A Python user porting code that catches TypeError on `series.astype(int)` needs our (eventual PyO3) binding to surface the same error category. If the Rust layer's error taxonomy diverges silently, the Python wrapper must map it manually, and any gap in that mapping lets a silent data-corruption bug through.

**Fix shape:**
1. Scan each of the 328 covered operations and file a sub-bead for the ones whose error paths are uncovered. Register the gap in a new `ERROR_CONFORMANCE.md` tracking doc.
2. Error-path fixture template — new `expected_error_category` field in PacketFixture alongside the existing `expected_error_contains`. Categories map to pandas-standard exception types: Type, Value, Key, Index, IO, Unsupported. The comparator checks category match (not text), per DISC-003.
3. Oracle support: `pandas_oracle.py` already captures pandas exceptions (confirmed via grep of `except Exception as e`). Extend `op_*` handlers to emit `{"expected_error": {...}, "expected_error_category": "TypeError"}` when the run raises.
4. Generate error-path fixtures bulk: for each op_class that has positive-path fixtures, synthesize 2-4 error fixtures (wrong dtype, missing column, empty input, negative integer where non-negative required, etc.). Target: lift error-path coverage from 7.7% to ~30% in the first sweep, 50%+ in a follow-up.
5. New comparator in fp-conformance lib.rs: `compare_expected_error_category(actual_err: &FrameError, expected: &ExpectedErrorCategory) -> bool` with a translation table from our error variants to the pandas category names.
6. DISCREPANCIES.md DISC-00N entry if category translation is impossible for some pair (e.g. our FrameError::CompatibilityRejected covers multiple pandas categories; subdivide the error type or document the many-to-one translation).

Blast radius: new enum in PacketFixture (+10 lines), new comparator (+30 lines), new fixtures (+200-400 files but tiny), new Python oracle exception-capture (+20 lines per impacted handler), ERROR_CONFORMANCE.md doc (new, ~40 lines).

Related:
- br-nato (RequirementLevel tagging) — most pandas error-paths are MUST (users catch specific types).
- br-urhy (oracle tests) — error-path oracle tests live naturally in oracle/tests/test_errors.py.
- br-zk1j (API gap matrix) — the matrix gains an "error-path covered: yes/no" column per op.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:12:09.481045789Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:12:09.481045789Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api-gap","conformance","errors","review"]}

+{"id":"frankenpandas-urhy","title":"[HIGH] conformance: pandas_oracle.py is 4985 LOC with zero test harness — oracle bugs produce silently-wrong fixtures","description":"Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill to the oracle-script dimension.

**Observation.** crates/fp-conformance/oracle/pandas_oracle.py is **4985 lines of Python** (`wc -l` verified). It contains:
- ~430 `op_*` handler functions (one per pandas operation under test)
- `dispatch()` — routes incoming JSON payloads to op handlers (line 4615+, hundreds of `if op in {...}` branches)
- Frame / Series / Index builders (dataframe_from_json, series_from_json, etc.)
- `normalize_*_frame` helpers that shape pandas output into the fixture JSON schema
- Exception translators from pandas error types to our expected_error_contains strings

**Gap.** There is NO test harness for the oracle itself. `find crates/fp-conformance/oracle/` returns exactly 2 files: `pandas_oracle.py` + a compiled __pycache__. No `test_pandas_oracle.py`, no pytest.ini, no doctests on the op handlers, no CI step that validates the oracle.

Confirmed: the ONLY validation the oracle gets is "produce output for each of the 1249 packet fixtures, check that Rust matches". That's a circular trust relationship — if the oracle emits the wrong answer for some op, Rust matches the wrong answer and every assertion passes green. The harness validates conformance-with-the-oracle, not conformance-with-pandas.

**Skill rule violated:** Pattern 1 Differential Testing requires that the reference implementation be trusted. Our "reference" is a 5k-line Python shim around pandas that may have bugs. Every undetected bug in a normalize_groupby_rolling_frame or op_dataframe_groupby_ohlc handler produces a permanently-wrong golden fixture.

**Concrete risk example.** br-ch4o (commit ac1550b) reverted br-r4au's tuple-emission for grouped rolling. The revert was driven by DataFrame shape, but imagine a subtler case: `normalize_groupby_resample_frame` uses pandas' `.resample('M').first()` and then some column-renaming logic. If the column-renaming silently drops a level on multi-index pandas output, Rust also drops that level to "pass" the fixture. The real pandas output retained it. Fixture encodes the drop. Users hit divergence.

**Severity.** HIGH — the oracle is the conformance spec's "source of truth." An untested 5k-line source of truth is itself a conformance risk, compounding with br-frankenpandas-d6xa (CI never runs live oracle) and br-frankenpandas-boyr (fixture drift vs pandas releases).

**Fix shape:**
1. `oracle/tests/test_pandas_oracle.py` — pytest suite. Per op handler, a minimal positive test (known input → known output matching vanilla pandas behavior the handler claims to preserve).
2. `oracle/tests/test_normalize.py` — unit tests on normalize_* helpers: assert that a pandas MultiIndex → JSON round-trips levels+names; that a datetime column → ISO 8601 string column is lossless for the range we care about; that NaN / NaT / None all normalize to the `{"kind": "null", ...}` shape documented in fixtures/README.md.
3. `oracle/tests/test_dispatch.py` — fuzz tests: generate random op_name + minimal payload, assert dispatch returns dict-with-expected-shape OR raises OracleError (not ValueError / KeyError / other Python-native exception escaping).
4. `oracle/conftest.py` + `oracle/requirements.txt` pinning pandas version (closes partial of br-8irq).
5. ci.yml runs `pytest crates/fp-conformance/oracle/tests/` after installing pandas (prerequisite: br-d6xa activates CI pandas install).
6. Document: every time a new op handler is added in pandas_oracle.py, the commit MUST include a matching test case (AGENTS.md addition).

Blast radius: 1 new tests/ directory (~1500 lines across 3 test files), 1 conftest.py, 1 requirements.txt, 2 ci.yml steps. Zero source code changes in either Rust or the oracle handlers themselves — purely additive test coverage.

Cost-benefit: oracle currently has 100% of the conformance trust weight and 0% of the test coverage. Adding ~30% coverage via positive tests catches the most common class of bug (wrong normalization) at trivial cost.

Depends on: br-d6xa (pandas in CI) as prerequisite for running these tests in CI.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:09:57.837995738Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:09:57.837995738Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","oracle","review","testing"]}

-{"id":"frankenpandas-zjme","title":"[HIGH] fuzz: no CI regression workflow or persistent corpus — fuzz fixes can silently regress","description":"Applying /testing-fuzzing skill audit to fuzz/ dir.

**Observed state:**
- 21 fuzz targets exist under fuzz/fuzz_targets/
- fuzz/Cargo.toml carries [[bin]] blocks for each
- .github/workflows/ci.yml has format, check, clippy, test, conformance, bench, phase2c, governance — NO fuzz step
- fuzz/corpus/ does not exist
- fuzz/artifacts/ does not exist

**Violates skill rules:**
- Rule 10 (Every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress) — no regression corpus
- Rule 14 (Fuzzing ROI follows a power law; invest in breadth) — harnesses exist but never re-run automatically
- Checklist "CI runs regression corpus on every PR" — not satisfied
- Checklist "Nightly continuous fuzzing scheduled" — not satisfied

**Concrete risk:** a crash found on day N and fixed on day N+1 is NOT locked in. Nothing on main exercises the fuzz targets after the fix. Any future regression that reintroduces the bug passes `cargo test` silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add fuzz/corpus/<target>/ with at least the empty seed + 3 boundary seeds per target (21 targets × 4 seeds = 84 files, small commit).
2. Add fuzz/artifacts/<target>/ (gitignored except for README.md listing known regression inputs once they arrive).
3. New CI job `fuzz-regression` that runs `cargo +nightly fuzz run <target> -- -runs=10000` for each target against the committed corpus. Exit non-zero on any new crash.
4. Optional: schedule a nightly GH Actions run that fuzzes each target for ~60s with the accumulated corpus, uploads coverage, opens a bead on new crashes (closes the loop).
5. Document the regression workflow in fuzz/README.md: found-a-crash → minimize (cargo fuzz tmin) → add to corpus → commit → CI locks it in.

No existing crashes known (test suite is 3161/0/13) — this is a pure infrastructure bead, not a bug bead. Priority HIGH because the risk is silent regression of any fuzz fix we've ever landed.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-22T18:44:51.397545485Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:32:31.094143504Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T20:32:31.091645714Z","close_reason":"Implemented","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ci","fuzz","infrastructure","review"]}

**Observed state:**
- 21 fuzz targets exist under fuzz/fuzz_targets/
- fuzz/Cargo.toml carries [[bin]] blocks for each
- .github/workflows/ci.yml has format, check, clippy, test, conformance, bench, phase2c, governance — NO fuzz step
- fuzz/corpus/ does not exist
- fuzz/artifacts/ does not exist

**Violates skill rules:**
- Rule 10 (Every crash artifact becomes a regression test or it WILL regress) — no regression corpus
- Rule 14 (Fuzzing ROI follows a power law; invest in breadth) — harnesses exist but never re-run automatically
- Checklist "CI runs regression corpus on every PR" — not satisfied
- Checklist "Nightly continuous fuzzing scheduled" — not satisfied

**Concrete risk:** a crash found on day N and fixed on day N+1 is NOT locked in. Nothing on main exercises the fuzz targets after the fix. Any future regression that reintroduces the bug passes `cargo test` silently.

**Fix shape:**
1. Add fuzz/corpus/<target>/ with at least the empty seed + 3 boundary seeds per target (21 targets × 4 seeds = 84 files, small commit).
2. Add fuzz/artifacts/<target>/ (gitignored except for README.md listing known regression inputs once they arrive).
3. New CI job `fuzz-regression` that runs `cargo +nightly fuzz run <target> -- -runs=10000` for each target against the committed corpus. Exit non-zero on any new crash.
4. Optional: schedule a nightly GH Actions run that fuzzes each target for ~60s with the accumulated corpus, uploads coverage, opens a bead on new crashes (closes the loop).
5. Document the regression workflow in fuzz/README.md: found-a-crash → minimize (cargo fuzz tmin) → add to corpus → commit → CI locks it in.

No existing crashes known (test suite is 3161/0/13) — this is a pure infrastructure bead, not a bug bead. +{"id":"frankenpandas-zk1j","title":"[HIGH] conformance: 328 ops covered vs pandas' ~1000+ API surface — no gap matrix answers 'what is not tested'","description":"Applying /testing-conformance-harnesses skill's "Before claiming conformance, prove it" rule + Coverage Accounting Matrix.

**Observation.** fixtures/packets/*.json covers **328 unique operation strings** (verified: grep '"operation"' + sort -u + wc -l → 328). pandas' public API surface is substantially larger:
- pandas Series: 300+ public methods and properties across arithmetic, selection, accessor chains (.str, .dt, .cat), aggregation, window, reshape, IO.
- pandas DataFrame: 400+ public methods.
- pandas GroupBy: 80+ public methods.
- pandas Index / MultiIndex / DatetimeIndex / CategoricalIndex: 200+ combined.
- pandas Rolling / Resample / ExpandingEwm: 40+ each.
- pandas Window via `.rolling()`, `.expanding()`, `.ewm()`: distinct method sets.
- Plus top-level functions: pd.concat, pd.merge, pd.merge_asof, pd.pivot_table, pd.crosstab, pd.cut, pd.qcut, pd.to_datetime, pd.to_timedelta, pd.read_*, pd.Series, pd.DataFrame, pd.MultiIndex.from_*, etc.

Rough total pandas public API: **~900-1200 callable surfaces**. Our 328-operation coverage is ~30% at the operation level, less when weighted by parameter-combination count.

**Gap.** Nowhere in the repo is there a document that answers "which pandas ops have zero fixtures?" There's no coverage-matrix generator that ingests pandas' published API listing (pd.Series.__dict__, pd.DataFrame.__dict__, etc.) and diffs against the operation strings our 1249 fixtures cover. README's COVERAGE.md lists 15 category rows with per-category counts, but (a) the counts are stale per br-n609 and (b) the doc doesn't enumerate missing operations per category.

**Skill rule violated:** "Before claiming conformance, prove it." The capability table in README.md:37 now reads "Conformance testing against pandas oracle | Yes (430+ packet suites, 1249 fixtures, all green)" — which sounds comprehensive but doesn't answer "of WHAT's tested vs WHAT pandas exposes."

**Severity.** HIGH — we cannot tell a user "feature X is at full pandas parity" vs "feature X has zero conformance coverage; it may look correct but is untested against the reference." Product positioning depends on this visibility.

**Fix shape:**
1. `scripts/gen_pandas_api_listing.py` — runs against a pinned pandas install, walks pd.{Series,DataFrame,GroupBy,Index,Rolling,...} introspectively, emits `artifacts/pandas_api_listing.json` with {class: [method_name, signature, docstring_first_line, ...]}.
2. `scripts/gen_coverage_matrix.py` — consumes artifacts/pandas_api_listing.json + grep of fixture operations, emits `COVERAGE_MATRIX.md` grouping:
     Full  → every call signature has fixtures
     Partial → some signatures fixtured, some not (list missing)
     Zero  → no fixtures
3. `scripts/gen_coverage_matrix.py` runs in CI (after pandas installs for br-d6xa) and fails the build if the matrix regresses (i.e. a Full-covered op loses all its fixtures without warning). Always-green on additive change.
4. Embed the Full/Partial/Zero counts at the top of README.md near the capability table.

Sample output sketch (what COVERAGE_MATRIX.md might look like):
  ### Series
  - Full: 127 methods (add, sub, mul, ..., str.*)
  - Partial: 38 methods (groupby — rolling covered, expanding not; resample — sum/mean covered, prod not)
  - Zero: 135 methods (pct_change, diff, dot, corr, cov, quantile with interpolation variants, ...)

Blast radius: 2 new Python scripts (~200 lines each), 1 new generated Markdown doc, 1 CI step (after br-d6xa lands pandas install). Zero Rust changes. Zero fixture changes.

Related:
- br-n609 (COVERAGE.md stale) — this bead supersedes the hand-maintained count with a generator.
- br-nato (RequirementLevel tagging) — level tagging enriches the matrix (each fixture's level becomes a cell attribute).
- br-boyr (pandas version pin) — the listing's pandas version is the anchor for Zero-category pandas APIs; when pandas adds new APIs, the matrix surfaces them.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:11:23.947981803Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-22T20:11:23.947981803Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api-gap","conformance","coverage","review"]}
